TITLE: LES POSSIBILITÉS D'ACCÈS À L'ÉDUCATION DANS LES ZONES RURALES
paragraph: 1. The authorities responsible for school administration must organize education for all children in rural areas that is equivalent in level to that provided to children in urban areas. In this regard, their responsibilities specifically include: a) conducting an inventory of educational needs in rural areas; b) determining the particular characteristics that the organization of education, curricula, and methods to be adopted for rural areas must have; c) implementing a plan of achievements (programs, school buildings, teaching staff, equipment, supplementary school activities, etc.); d) allocating the necessary funds and ensuring their distribution, according to identified needs, between rural and urban areas; e) ensuring equal access for children from rural areas to guidance services and post-primary studies. It is desirable that the responsible authorities keep representatives of public opinion, particularly parents’ associations and teachers’ organizations, informed of their intentions and possibly involve them in their work;

low_level_thinking.Infrastructure & resources: The paragraph explicitly mentions school buildings, teaching staff, equipment, and supplementary activities, aligning with 'School buildings and physical infrastructure' and 'Teaching and learning materials'. 'Transport and boarding' is included due to the emphasis on equal access to post-primary studies, implying transportation needs. 'ICT and digital infrastructure' and 'WASH facilities' are not mentioned. 'Classrooms and learning environments' is too vague compared to specific references.
low_level_thinking.Stakeholder focus: The paragraph emphasizes responsibilities of authorities (government/policy makers) for rural education planning. Parents' associations and teachers' organizations are explicitly mentioned as stakeholders to involve, covering parents and educators. Learners are implied through 'children in rural areas' but not directly addressed. Civil society/NGOs and private sector are not mentioned.
